title: Know Your Assumptions: Adaptive Algorithm Selection for Robust Causal Discovery via Data-Driven Diagnostics
description: We propose AACD (Assumption-Adaptive Causal Discovery), a framework that runs data-driven diagnostic tests to assess which statistical assumptions hold in a given dataset, then uses these diagnostics to adaptively weight and aggregate outputs from multiple causal discovery algorithms spanning different assumption families. Unlike prior ensemble approaches that combine algorithms via uniform voting, AACD weights algorithms by empirically measured assumption compatibility, with sample-size-dependent confidence weighting. We validate on both synthetic data (including realistic semi-heterogeneous settings) and real-world benchmarks (Sachs, Alarm, Child, Tuebingen pairs) to demonstrate that diagnostics provide genuine signal beyond synthetic artifacts.
motivation: Every causal discovery algorithm makes assumptions (linearity, non-Gaussianity, faithfulness, additive noise). When assumptions are violated, algorithms fail unpredictably. Practitioners face an impossible choice: pick one algorithm and hope its assumptions hold. Recent work confirms that no single algorithm dominates across all data regimes, and hyperparameter/algorithm choices dramatically affect outcomes. Existing ensemble approaches aggregate by voting, bootstrap stability, or DAG topology classification -- none use data-driven assumption diagnostics to guide weighting. Furthermore, prior ensemble evaluations have been limited to synthetic data, leaving open whether their advantages are artifacts of controlled data generation.
proposed_approach: AACD operates in three stages: (1) DIAGNOSE: compute statistical tests on variable pairs to assess linearity (polynomial fit comparison), non-Gaussianity (Shapiro-Wilk on residuals), additive noise model fit (HSIC with 200 permutations on subsamples), faithfulness proximity (partial correlations up to k=2), and homoscedasticity (Breusch-Pagan test); (2) DISCOVER: run multiple causal discovery algorithms (PC, GES, BOSS, DirectLiNGAM, ANM) from different assumption families; (3) AGGREGATE: weight each algorithm's output per-edge using sigmoid functions tuned via grid search on held-out synthetic data, multiplied by sample-size-dependent confidence weights. Acyclicity is enforced via a minimum feedback arc set relaxation (Eades heuristic). We validate on synthetic data with both uniform (25/25/25/25) and realistic semi-heterogeneous (70/30) mechanism mixing, plus 3 real-world benchmarks (Sachs protein signaling, Alarm, Child) and the Tuebingen cause-effect pairs.

related_work.title: Evaluation of Algorithm Selection and Ensemble Methods for Causal Discovery
related_work.relevance: Most directly related: evaluates ensemble methods for combining causal discovery algorithms via voting without assumption diagnosis. AACD differs by introducing data-driven diagnostics for principled per-dataset algorithm weighting.

related_work.title: Assumption violations in causal discovery and the robustness of score matching
related_work.authors: Montagna, Mastakouri, Eulig, Noceti, Rosasco, Janzing, Aragam, Locatello
related_work.relevance: Benchmarks causal discovery under assumption violations. We build on their finding that assumptions matter but go further by proposing an adaptive framework.

related_work.title: A Diagnostic Tool for Functional Causal Discovery
related_work.relevance: Proposes CDDR diagnostic for bivariate causal discovery. We extend the diagnostic concept to multivariate settings and use diagnostics proactively for algorithm selection.

related_work.title: Hyperparameters in Structure Learning
related_work.relevance: Shows hyperparameter choices dramatically affect causal discovery. Motivates aggregating across algorithms rather than relying on one.

related_work.title: Scalable and flexible two-phase ensemble algorithms for causality discovery
related_work.relevance: Develops scalable ensemble via data partitioning and algorithm combining. Focuses on scalability not assumption-aware weighting.

related_work.title: Supervised Ensemble-based Causal DAG Selection
related_work.relevance: Uses supervised classification on DAG topologies to select best DAG. AACD differs by weighting based on assumption compatibility.

related_work.title: GaussDetect-LiNGAM: Causal Direction Identification without Gaussianity test
related_work.relevance: Diagnoses Gaussianity to decide LiNGAM applicability, but only for bivariate cases.

related_work.title: Efficient Causal Structure Learning via Modular Subgraph Integration (VISTA)
related_work.authors: Sun, Tian, Zhou, Zhang, Li, Liu
related_work.relevance: Ensemble aggregation via weighted voting on local subgraphs within a single method family.

related_work.title: Bootstrap aggregation and confidence measures to improve time series causal discovery
related_work.relevance: Bootstrap aggregation for a single algorithm. We aggregate across fundamentally different algorithms using assumption diagnostics.

related_work.title: Power analysis for causal discovery
related_work.venue: International Journal of Data Science and Analytics 2024
related_work.relevance: First power analysis framework showing sample size x effect size interaction. Informs our diagnostic confidence weighting.

related_work.title: Fast Scalable and Accurate Discovery of DAGs Using the Best Order Score Search and Grow-Shrink Trees (BOSS)
related_work.relevance: State-of-the-art permutation-based causal discovery algorithm included in our ensemble.

related_work.title: A Linear Non-Gaussian Acyclic Model for Causal Discovery
related_work.relevance: Foundational functional causal discovery method (LiNGAM) that leverages non-Gaussianity.

related_work.title: Causal protein-signaling networks derived from multiparameter single-cell data
related_work.relevance: Canonical real-world benchmark for causal discovery: 11 proteins, 17 known edges, ~5400 flow cytometry observations.

related_work.title: Distinguishing cause from effect using observational data: methods and benchmarks
related_work.relevance: Provides 108 real-world bivariate cause-effect pairs for evaluating pairwise causal direction methods.

related_work.title: A comprehensive review of causal discovery algorithms
related_work.relevance: Notes that dataset characteristics significantly impact algorithm selection, a problem we directly address.

related_work.title: How PC-based Methods Err
related_work.relevance: Characterizes error patterns in constraint-based methods, informing when to trust PC-family algorithms.

hypothesis: An assumption-adaptive ensemble that diagnoses data characteristics (linearity, non-Gaussianity, additive noise fit, faithfulness proximity) and weights causal discovery algorithms by assumption compatibility -- with sample-size-dependent confidence weighting -- will achieve significantly lower structural Hamming distance and higher F1 score than any single algorithm or naive ensemble, both on synthetic data with heterogeneous mechanisms (including realistic 70/30 semi-heterogeneous mixing) and on real-world benchmarks (Sachs, Alarm, Child) where data assumptions are unknown a priori.
success_criteria: AACD achieves statistically significantly lower SHD than all individual algorithms on heterogeneous-mixed synthetic data (Wilcoxon signed-rank test, p < 0.05)
AACD achieves lower SHD than naive (equal-weight) ensemble and stability-weighted ensemble on heterogeneous-mixed data
On at least 2 of 3 real-world benchmarks (Sachs, Alarm, Child), AACD matches or outperforms the best individual algorithm and outperforms naive ensemble
AACD's advantage persists on semi-heterogeneous (70/30) data, not just fully-heterogeneous (25/25/25/25) data
AACD's edge confidence scores are reasonably calibrated (expected calibration error < 0.15)
Ablation study confirms assumption diagnostics drive improvement beyond mere algorithm diversity
AACD with confidence weighting never performs worse than naive ensemble even at n=500
